entry.title: Header Freight condition in Purchase order
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

I have a procurement scenario where the Purchase Order is created for the actual material supplier, while freight charges are maintained through separate freight condition types with a different freight supplier.

In this case, the PO document currency is maintained as USD, and the freight condition type is entered in QAR currency. The requirement is to post separate invoices for the material supplier and the freight supplier, with each invoice adopting the respective currency maintained in the PO:

  • Material Supplier Invoice – USD
  • Freight Supplier Invoice – QAR

Could you please advise on the configuration settings and process steps required to achieve this scenario? Specifically, I would like to understand how SAP handles multiple currencies for planned delivery costs and whether any additional configuration is required to ensure that the supplier invoices are posted in their respective currencies.

Your guidance would be highly appreciated.

Best Regards

Dinesh

entry.title: Re: Employee as BP
entry.content: Yes, you can assign identical number ranges to the BP grouping and the related Customer/Supplier account groups for employees. The best practice is to use a dedicated internal number range for this grouping, so that the BP number remains consistent across all roles. This ensures that when the same employee acts as both Customer and Supplier, the account numbers align with the BP number. For future SuccessFactors integration, keep an identifier field reserved (e.g., External ID) to map employee records without creating duplicates.

entry.title: Re: Problem reinstalling SAP ASE
entry.content: If the previous installation wasn't fully removed, there might still be old ASE files, ports, or shared memory segments causing conflicts during the server build step. I've seen installs hang because of leftover configuration from an earlier instance. You could try checking whether any old ASE processes or resources are still present before reinstalling. Also, if you can share the specific error from the build phase, that would help narrow it down. By the way, I've run into similar issues with platform migrations on phonexa, where a partial cleanup caused the new deployment to get stuck unexpectedly.

entry.title: Re: Header Freight condition in Purchase order
entry.content: Yes, SAP supports this scenario. You need to configure freight condition types as planned delivery costs, assign them to the freight vendor, and allow currency entry in QAR. During invoice posting, the system will generate separate invoices in USD for the material supplier and QAR for the freight supplier, without additional currency customizing.

entry.title: Handling Large Volumes of Data Between SAP CPI and S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Most SAP CPI integrations work perfectly in development. Then you hit production — and everything changes.

500,000 sales orders. 2 million material records. Suddenly you're seeing timeouts, memory errors, and an S/4 HANA system that slows down for every other user. I've been there. Here's what actually works.

The Real Problem Is End-to-End, Not Just CPI

High-volume data processing touches four layers simultaneously — the ABAP database, the OData gateway, the Cloud Connector tunnel, and the CPI runtime. Most teams focus only on CPI and wonder why tuning iFlow settings doesn't fix anything. The answer is usually one layer below.

Layer 1 — Fix S/4 HANA First

Before writing a single iFlow step, go to your S/4 HANA system and check one thing: are your filter fields indexed?

When CPI filters OData results by LastChangeDateTime, the ABAP layer runs a SQL WHERE clause against the underlying table. Without a database index on that field, the database scans every single row — all 10 million of them — just to return 500 records.

That's the difference between a 0.3-second query and a 15-second one. Across 2,000 pages of data, that's 10 minutes versus 8 hours — while blocking ABAP work processes for every other user on the system.

Check indexes in SE11 → table name → Indexes tab. If your filter field isn't there, raise it with your BASIS team before anything else.

Also verify:

  • OData service is active in SICF and /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  • A dedicated technical user exists with the correct S_SERVICE role
  • Test the full OData call with $top=500 in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT before building

Layer 2 — Tune the Cloud Connector

The default connection pool in SAP Cloud Connector is 5 connections. For high-volume integration, that's a bottleneck you can't see — requests queue silently and your iFlow slows without throwing errors.

Increase the pool to 20–50 connections. Enable HTTP Keep-Alive. Enable GZIP compression on the system mapping — this reduces OData payload size by 60–70% and takes load off the tunnel significantly.

Layer 3 — Stop Fighting the OData Adapter

Here's the mistake I see most often: developers manually add $top=500&$skip=0 to the query string, then also enable Process in Pages in the OData adapter. The adapter adds its own pagination on top — values conflict, and you get duplicated pages or an infinite loop on page 1.

The CPI OData Receiver Adapter handles pagination natively. Enable Process in Pages, set your page size (200–500 records), and put only this in your query options:

  • $select — fetch only the fields you need (cuts payload by 60–80%)
  • $filter — for delta loading by date or key field

That's it. No $top. No $skip. Let the adapter do its job.

The Delta Load Pattern — Non-Negotiable at Scale

Never run full loads on recurring integrations with more than 10,000 records. Instead:

  1. Save the run timestamp to a CPI DataStore after each successful execution
  2. On the next run, filter with $filter=LastChangeDateTime gt datetime'<saved timestamp>'
  3. Process only what changed — update the timestamp on success

This turns millions of records into hundreds per run.

The Pattern That Holds at Production Scale

Scheduler

  → Read timestamp from DataStore

  → OData Adapter (Process in Pages ON)

  → Splitter (one record per message)

  → Process + write to target

  → Save new timestamp to DataStore

Simple. Resilient. Scalable.

Here is the skeletal working flow between S/4 OnPrem to CPI to pull data using Get.

  • Timer: OnDeployment
  • Content Modifier4:

Use timestamp variable to fetch data based on specific specific date

property timestamp to be given based on the abap field timestamp like(20000203062111)

 

 

  • Add looping process call
  1. Expression Type : Non XML
  2. Condition Expression: use ${property.Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords} is a CPI-internal exchange property that the OData Receiver Adapter sets automatically after each page call.
  3. Max. Numbers of Iterations: 10 (depends on the payload use this effectively, if you expect to receive 1lakh record use 100 to 120)
  4. Action When Max Iterations Reached: Throw Exception

 

 

 

  • add Request/Reply pallet to connect ODATA receiver adapter.
  • use ODATA V2 receiver Adapter

 

 

  1. For my testing using Page Size as 30
  2. Process in pages : checked

In the query there is no need of using $TOP and $SKIP, just make sure these are enabled in ABAP end that’s it,

CPI will take care in calling the looping process call until
Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = ‘false’

 

 

 

 

  • Add ContentModifier2

Every new OData call replaces in.body completely. By the time the loop ends, you only have the last page — all previous pages are lost.

You are accumulating each page response into a property before the next OData call overwrites in.body.

Property name: appendPayload

SourceType: Expression
SourceValue: ${property.appendPayload}${in.body}

 

 

  • Add Content Modifier 3
  1. Under Main integration process add the property appendPayload to the Message body as ${property.appendPayload} to send it to target.
  2. Use all the transformational pallet functions under the Local Integration Process where ODATA is calling to S/4, so that pallet functions process data page wise, at the end you can append payload using the below approach.

 

Lets deploy and monitor

  • CPI Helper

 

  1. CamelLoopIndex : 3

is a built-in CPI property that tells you which iteration the loop is currently on.

  1. For the last looping index got Receiver1.OData.hasMoreRecords = false, next loop will end the process.
  2. SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData: is the property CPI uses internally to track and inject the $skip value into every OData page request — it increments automatically so you never need to calculate it yourself.

 

 

SkipRecords_Receiver1.OData

 

High-volume SAP CPI integration isn't complicated once you understand each layer's role. The teams that struggle are the ones treating it as a black box — tuning CPI while a missing database index silently kills performance.

Know your stack. Test each layer. Let the tools do what they're designed to do.

Have you faced high-volume integration challenges in your SAP landscape? Drop a comment — I'd love to hear your approach.

#SAP #SAPCPI #S4HANA #CloudIntegration #SAPIntegration #OData #CloudConnector #SAPDeveloper #IntegrationArchitecture

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA PP Certification – What is the new system...
entry.content: Hi Jan, Thank you again for taking the time to answer my previous questions. Your comments have been extremely helpful and have probably given me more insight into the new SBA format than anything else I have found so far. I had a few follow-up questions, particularly around the 24-hour preparation period, as this is something I am still unclear about: 1. When you mention using the 24 hours to study the task, does SAP provide the complete scenario and all tasks upfront before the 3-hour execution window begins? 2. During that 24-hour period, can you access the exam system and inspect the existing data, or can you only review the scenario documentation? 3. Are you able to create your own notes, checklists and execution plan during the preparation period? 4. When the 3-hour timer starts, are you already working in the same SAP environment, or does SAP provide a separate system for the actual exam execution? 5. Looking back, how did you use the 24-hour period, and what would you do differently if you were taking the exam again? I was also interested in your comment that the exam was roughly one-third Customizing, one-third Master Data and one-third Execution. 6. By "Customizing", are we mainly talking about settings within the Material Master (MRP Type, Procurement Type, Strategy Group, Lot Size, etc.), or is there any actual IMG/SPRO configuration involved? 7. For the Master Data section, were you required to create objects such as Materials, BOMs, Routings, Work Centres and Production Versions from scratch, or were you mostly modifying existing data? 8. Do mistakes in earlier tasks affect later tasks? For example, if a strategy group or production version is set incorrectly at the start, can that impact the remainder of the scenario and potentially cost marks throughout the exam? 9. Did SAP provide any breakdown of where marks were lost, or was only the final percentage shown? 10. Finally, if you sat the exam again tomorrow, what would be the three biggest pieces of advice you would give yourself beforehand? I really appreciate your help and experience with this. The information you've shared has been incredibly valuable. Kind regards, Paul

entry.title: Re: product Revision level
entry.content:
Dear @Noddy_R,

No, in Public Cloud, Product / Material Revision Level is not designed as an unrestricted free-text field comparable to the ECC External Revision Levels approach

SAP confirms two important points:

  1. The Change Number must be populated before the Create Revision Level function is available in Manage Product Master Data
  2. Revision levels are determined from the configuration activity / SSCUI Define Revision Levels for Materials

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3259773

Therefore, removing all maintained revision levels should not be used as a supported way to allow any revision level value. The clean-core Public Cloud approach is to define the required revision level values in configuration, for example P1 - P5 for preproduction and the required later numeric or alphanumeric revision codes

For the post-migration mass load the correct technical approach is to use API_PRODUCT with the Product Change Master relationship. SAP confirms that updating the material revision level with a change number via API_PRODUCT must be done using a $batch request to:

Product('product number')/_ProductChangeMaster

with the values Product, ChangeNumber and RevisionLevel in the payload

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3709249

Do not use API_CHANGEMASTER;v=0002 for this requirement. SAP confirms that revision level update through the Change Master API entity A_ChangeMstrObMgReMaterial is not supported, even if the API technically returns a successful 201 response

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2948975

If you receives the message Revision level cannot be created for material the Change Number setup should also be checked. SAP explains that this can happen when the Gen New flag is set for object type Material in the Change Master the resolution is to deselect this flag for the relevant Change Number

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2853498

Summary:

Deleting all revision levels is not a supported way to make revision levels unrestricted. In Public Cloud the safe approach is to maintain the allowed revision level values, assign them with a valid Change Number and use API_PRODUCT / Product Change Master with $batch for mass loading

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Wrong setup time and produce time in combined order in ePPDS
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are facing an issue in SAP PP/DS (Embedded PP/DS, S/4HANA 2023) related to order scheduling after combining production orders using transaction MILL_OC.

Scenario:

  1. A planned order is converted into a production order.
  2. When we manually reschedule the production order to an alternative resource/machine, the setup time and run time remain unchanged, which is the expected behavior.
  3. However, when multiple production orders are combined using MILL_OC, the resulting combined order shows different setup and run times between the main resource and alternative resources when we reschedule via DS Board.
  4. According to us all the main and alternate resource have same setup details in master data and classifications. 

Has anyone experienced mode inconsistencies between the original orders and the combined order ?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

Br

@s_sudhirkumar

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Solid overview — and having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I'd add one critical nuance that most implementation guides skip: the go-live week is where Hyderabad SMBs lose the most money, and it's almost never a technical issue. It's a data readiness and visibility gap.

Here's what I see repeatedly:

1. Master data is "good enough" on cutover night — and wrong for months after.
Teams migrate from Tally or legacy spreadsheets and skip proper validation under time pressure. Three weeks later, inventory reports are unreliable because unit of measure conversions weren't standardised, or item master cost prices weren't updated. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for at least 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved. No exceptions.

2. Real-time operational visibility is treated as a post-go-live "nice to have."
This is the one that costs the most. Teams go live, start processing transactions, and only discover blind spots at month-end — when it's too late to course-correct. The smarter approach: define your key operational KPIs (inventory turnover, batch cost per unit, cashflow position, supplier quality flags) before go-live, and verify you can pull them on Day 1. If you can't see your numbers in real time, you're flying blind during the most critical window.

For pharma and manufacturing clients specifically, batch cost gap analysis has become a non-negotiable pre-go-live check — understanding what your system says a batch costs versus what it actually cost in the old system. Reconciling that gap before go-live prevents weeks of painful post-implementation cleanup. We've started using AI-assisted analysis (via TekAI, our SAP B1 operations layer) to surface these gaps automatically rather than running manual reconciliations. The time savings on cutover preparation alone is significant.

3. No rollback decision tree.
If something breaks on go-live night, who decides to push through vs. rollback? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos. Document the decision criteria in advance — what conditions trigger a rollback, who has authority, and what the recovery path looks like.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run before go-live where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful, yes. But far cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover.

Happy to share our go-live checklist template for SAP B1 SMB implementations if useful.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs | TekAI: real-time operations intelligence on SAP B1

entry.title: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
In this Blog I have explained in detail on how to create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI.

Steps:

  • Create a new Master Table name as ZMAST_DET_TEST3 using transaction SE11.
  • Create the Key Attribute Structure in SE11 for the above Table.
  • Create the Attribute Structure in SE11.
  • Create the Table Type for the above Attribute Structure In se11.
  • Create a API Class ZCL_DETAILS_API_3 with below methods:
      • CREATE_DETAILS_3
      • SAVE_DETAILS_3
      • GET_DETAILS_3
      • CHANGE_DETAILS_3
  • Add the Global Attribute GT_DETAILS_3 TYPE REF TO ZATTR_MAST3_TAB.
  • Create a Custom Genil Object class ZCL_DETAILS_MODEL and update the Super Class as CL_WCF_GENIL_ABSTR_COMPONENT and redefine the below Methods:
    • IF_GENIL_APPL_ALTERNATIVE_DSIL~SAVE_OBJECTS
    • IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~CREATE_OBJECTS
    • IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~GET_OBJECTS
    • IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~MODIFY_OBJECTS
  • Create the Root Object in TCode GENIL_MODEL_BROWSER
  • Make the entry in View Cluster CRMVC_GIL_APPDEF in TCode sm34

Detailed Explanation:

Create a new Master Table name as ZMAST_DET_TEST3 using transaction SE11.

Create the Key Attribute Structure in SE11 for the above Table

Create the Attribute Structure in SE11

Create the Table Type for the above Attribute Structure In se11:

Create a API Class ZCL_DETAILS_API_3 with below methods:

  • CREATE_DETAILS_3
  • SAVE_DETAILS_3
  • GET_DETAILS_3
  • CHANGE_DETAILS_3

 

 

 

 

 

Add the Global Attribute GT_DETAILS_3 TYPE REF TO ZATTR_MAST3_TAB.

 

 

 

Method CREATE_DETAILS_3:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Method SAVE_DETAILS_3:

 

 

 

 

 

 

Method GET_DETAILS_3:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Method CHANGE_DETAILS_3:

 

 

 

Create a Custom Genil Object class ZCL_DETAILS_MODEL and update the Super Class as CL_WCF_GENIL_ABSTR_COMPONENT and redefine the below Methods:

  • IF_GENIL_APPL_ALTERNATIVE_DSIL~SAVE_OBJECTS
  • IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~CREATE_OBJECTS
  • IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~GET_OBJECTS
  • IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~MODIFY_OBJECTS

entry.title: Re: We would like to modify the PDF layout generated from...
entry.content:
Hi Olivia,

To clarify the technical behavior of the system: when you use the standard "Export as" button and choose PDF directly from the Fiori table toolbar in the Manage Customer Line Items app, SAP does not use an underlying PDF template.

This button triggers a generic system export function that is designed to generate flat data. While it retains your active filters and basic sorting, it systematically ignores UI-level visual grouping or subtotals. Because there is no Adobe Form or template linked to this specific button, it cannot be modified via Key User Extensibility. See Sap Note 2652146 

Regarding your suggestion to build a custom CDS View: building a custom report alone will not solve this specific PDF layout issue, as the standard export button in a custom report will still flatten the data in the exact same way. See suggestion number 3.

To achieve your goal, I see three approaches, ranked from least to most effort:

1. The Standard Workaround (No-Code): Instead of exporting to PDF, export the flat list to a Spreadsheet (Excel). You can then quickly apply Excel's native Subtotal function or a Pivot Table to group the data by Customer ID and calculate the summaries. Finally, "Save as PDF" directly from Excel. While this adds a manual step, it requires zero development effort, and once your Excel is fine-tuned, it does not take so much time to generate such a report.

2. Use SAP Output Control (Correspondence): If a pre-formatted PDF directly from the system is strictly required, do not use the UI export button. Instead, trigger a standard Correspondence (such as an Open Items List). This process does use SAP Output Control and an underlying Adobe Form template (.xdp). You can download the standard template via the Maintain Form Templates app, apply your specific grouping and subtotal logic within Adobe LiveCycle Designer, and upload it as a custom form.

3. Full Custom Application: If you proceed with the custom CDS View route, you would need to build a Custom Fiori Application and explicitly integrate it with SAP Output Management (setting up BRF+ rules) to trigger a custom Adobe Form. This represents significantly more development and maintenance effort compared to the options above.

Hope it helps,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method CREATE_DETAILS_3. *************************************************************************************

* In this Method we update the details to be Updated into the Database Table *************************************************************************************

DATA : lv_data3 like line OF GT_DETAILS_3.

* First we lock the table

CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZMAST_DET_TEST3'

EXPORTING MODE_ZMAST_DET_TEST3 = 'E'

EXCEPTIONS

FOREIGN_LOCK = 1

SYSTEM_FAILURE = 2

OTHERS = 3 .

IF sy-subrc <> 0.

* Implement suitable error handling here

ENDIF.

* Assign the various values

lv_data3-guid = is_det_key_3-guid.

lv_data3-ind_number = is_det_key_3-IND_NUMBER.

lv_data3-new1 = 'C'.

* Appending to the Internal Table

Append lv_data3 to gt_details_3.

rv_success = 'X'.

endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method SAVE_DETAILS_3.
****************************************************************************
* In this method we Save the details to the Database
****************************************************************************
DATA : wa_det_3 TYPE ZMAST_DET_TEST3.
DATA : lv_success TYPE abap_bool.

FIELD-SYMBOLS: <details_attr_n> LIKE LINE OF gt_details_3.

lv_success = 'X'.
* We read the Interal table to get the values
READ TABLE gt_details_3 ASSIGNING <details_attr_n> WITH KEY guid = det_key_3-guid
                                                                                                           IND_NUMBER = det_key_3-ind_number.
* Here we check the value of sy-syubrc
CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
* Here we check if the Attribute is Create or Modify
CASE <details_attr_n>-new1.
WHEN 'C' OR 'M'.
* Move the values
MOVE-CORRESPONDING <details_attr_n> TO wa_det_3.
* Update the Database Table
MODIFY ZMAST_DET_TEST3 FROM wa_det_3.
WHEN OTHERS.
ENDCASE.

CALL FUNCTION 'DEQUEUE_EZMAST_DET_TEST3'
EXPORTING
MODE_ZMAST_DET_TEST3 = 'E'
guid                                             = det_key_3-guid
IND_NUMBER                          = det_key_3-IND_NUMBER.
* Clearing the Structure
CLEAR det_key_3.
endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method GET_DETAILS_3.
**************************************************************************
* In this method we get the details
**************************************************************************
FIELD-SYMBOLS <data> LIKE LINE OF gt_details_3.
* Checking if the structure is not initial
IF is_det_key_3 IS NOT INITIAL.

* We read from Buffer.
READ TABLE gt_details_3 WITH KEY  guid                = is_det_key_3-guid
                                                                       ind_number = is_det_key_3-ind_number
                                            ASSIGNING <data>.
ELSE.
READ TABLE gt_details_3 WITH KEY new1 = 'C' ASSIGNING <data>.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
RETURN.
ENDIF.
ENDIF.

IF sy-subrc = 0.
IF <data>-new1 EQ 'C' OR <data>-new1 EQ 'M'.
* We move the values to the structure
MOVE-CORRESPONDING <data> TO es_det_attr_3.
RETURN.
ENDIF.
ENDIF.
endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method CHANGE_DETAILS_3.
*******************************************************************
* In this method we Change the details
*******************************************************************
* Data Declarations
FIELD-SYMBOLS : <old> TYPE simple,
<new> TYPE simple,
<line> TYPE ZATTR_MAST3,
<name> TYPE name_komp.
* Read the internal table
READ TABLE gt_details_3 WITH KEY guid = is_det_attr_3-guid
                                                                      ind_number = is_det_attr_3-ind_number  ASSIGNING <line> .

* Check the value ofsy-subrc
CHECK sy-subrc IS INITIAL.
* Looping at the Internal table
LOOP AT it_names ASSIGNING <name>.
ASSIGN COMPONENT <name> OF STRUCTURE <line> TO <old>.
CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
ASSIGN COMPONENT <name> OF STRUCTURE is_det_attr_3 TO <new>.
* Checking the value of sy-subrc
CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
<old> = <new>.
ENDLOOP.
<line>-new1 = 'M'.
rv_success ='X'.
endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method IF_GENIL_APPL_ALTERNATIVE_DSIL~SAVE_OBJECTS.
******************************************************************
* In this method we Save the Objects to the Database and is triggered when the user clicks *'Save' on the UI
******************************************************************
DATA lv_det_key_3 TYPE ZATTR_MAST_DET_TEST_KEY3.
FIELD-SYMBOLS <lo_object> TYPE crmt_genil_obj_inst_line.
* Here we loop at the Internal table
LOOP AT ct_object_list ASSIGNING <lo_object>.
* We check for the Object Instance.
CASE <lo_object>-object_name.
WHEN 'Details3'.
TRY.
CALL METHOD cl_crm_genil_container_tools=>get_key_from_object_id(
EXPORTING
iv_object_name = <lo_object>-object_name
iv_object_id = <lo_object>-object_id
IMPORTING
es_key = lv_det_key_3 ).
CATCH cx_crm_genil_general_error .
ENDTRY.
* Here we call the Custom API to Save the data.
CALL METHOD zcl_details_api_3=>save_details_3
CHANGING
det_key_3 = lv_det_key_3.
* Check if the variable is Initial
IF lv_det_key_3 IS INITIAL.
<lo_object>-success = abap_true.
ENDIF.
ENDCASE.
ENDLOOP.
endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~CREATE_OBJECTS.
*************************************************************************************
* Here we instantiate new business objects at runtime in the GenIL buffer
*************************************************************************************
TYPES : BEGIN OF ty_det3,
IND_NUMBER TYPE numc5,
END OF ty_det3.

DATA : lit_det3     TYPE TABLE OF ty_det3.
DATA : lv_key3     TYPE ZATTR_MAST_DET_TEST_KEY3,
            lv_guid3    TYPE crmt_object_guid,
           lv_success TYPE char1,
           wa3              TYPE ty_det3,
            lv_count     TYPE i VALUE 0.

* Get the Name Pairs table and fill the structure
fill_struct_from_nvp_tab( EXPORTING it_parameters = it_parameters
                                                  CHANGING  cs_parameter = lv_key3 ).
* Here we Create the Guid.
CALL FUNCTION 'GUID_CREATE'
IMPORTING
ev_guid_16 = lv_guid3.

* Here we create the data for the New Entry into the table
SELECT IND_NUMBER FROM ZMAST_DET_TEST3 INTO TABLE lit_det3.
* Sort the Internal table
SORT lit_det3 DESCENDING.
* Checking if the Internal table is not initial
IF lit_det3 IS NOT INITIAL.
* Here we loop at the Internal table
LOOP AT lit_det3 INTO wa3.
* Here we Increment the count by 1
   lv_count = wa3-IND_NUMBER + 1.
EXIT.
ENDLOOP.
ELSE.
lv_count = 1.
ENDIF.
* Here we assign the values
lv_key3-guid = lv_guid3.
lv_key3-IND_NUMBER = lv_count.

* Here we call the API Class fills the the global attribute.
CALL METHOD zcl_details_api_3=>create_details_3
EXPORTING
is_det_key_3 = lv_key3
IMPORTING
rv_success = lv_success.

* Here we add the object to Data Container
IF lv_success IS NOT INITIAL.
iv_root_list->add_object( iv_object_name = iv_object_name
is_object_key = lv_key3 ).
ENDIF.
endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Lack of History Tracking for Key Figure Changes in Ob...
entry.content:
Hi, correct. This is working as designed. 

In reporting the business wants to see the data as of now. Not any deltas, or versioning. In that case you can create snapshots as versions. Alternative is to create snapshots only for changed measures. But that will be more difficult to reconcile, but if you use DSP just as EDI, then this is possible. Another method would be CDC. 

Even in Planning Scenarios, the Versions are kind of snapshots. 

Depends on the reason / root cause why do you need to version the measures. I would suggest snapshots. This way you are always sure to have a fully independent version for all measures, attributes, ... 

Martin

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~GET_OBJECTS.
*************************************************************************
* Triggered by the UI layer to read and fetch the attributes of specific
* Business Object Layer (BOL) entities
*************************************************************************
DATA : lv_key3        TYPE ZATTR_MAST_DET_TEST_KEY3,
             lv_det_att3 TYPE ZATTR_MAST3,
             lv_root          TYPE REF TO if_genil_cont_root_object.
* Here we get the first object.
lv_root = iv_root_list->get_first( ).
* Here we get the Key
lv_root->get_key( IMPORTING es_key = lv_key3 ).
IF lv_root->check_attr_requested( ) = abap_true.
* Here we call the Custom API class to get the attributes.
zcl_details_api_3=>get_details_3( EXPORTING is_det_key_3 = lv_key3
IMPORTING es_det_attr_3 = lv_det_att3 ).

IF lv_det_att3 IS NOT INITIAL.
* Set the attributes
lv_root->set_attributes( lv_det_att3 ).
lv_root = iv_root_list->get_next( ).
ENDIF.
ENDIF.
endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Steps to Create Root Object in SAP CRM Web UI
entry.content:
method IF_GENIL_APPL_INTLAY~MODIFY_OBJECTS.
************************************************************************
* This method is invoked whenever a user creates, deletes, or changes
* attribute values on the UI
************************************************************************
DATA : lv_root                           TYPE REF TO if_genil_container_object,
             lv_props                       TYPE REF TO if_genil_obj_attr_properties,
             lv_changed_objects  TYPE                crmt_genil_obj_instance,
             lv_det_attr3                 TYPE ZATTR_MAST3,
             lt_changed_attr          TYPE crmt_attr_name_tab,
             lv_det_key3                 TYPE ZATTR_MAST_DET_TEST_KEY3,
             lv_success                    TYPE abap_bool,
             lv_change                      TYPE crmt_genil_attr_property.

CHECK iv_root_list IS BOUND.
* Here we get the first object from Container.
lv_root = iv_root_list->get_first( ).
IF lv_root->get_delta_flag( ) IS NOT INITIAL.
* Here we get name of the object.
CASE lv_root->get_name( ).
WHEN 'Details3'.
* Here we get the Attributes Property Object
lv_props = lv_root->get_attr_props_obj( ).
CALL METHOD lv_props->get_name_tab_4_property
EXPORTING
iv_property = if_genil_obj_attr_properties=>modified
IMPORTING
et_names = lt_changed_attr.
* Here we get the values
lv_root->get_key( IMPORTING es_key = lv_det_key3 ).
lv_root->get_attributes( IMPORTING es_attributes = lv_det_attr3 ).
* Here we move the values
MOVE-CORRESPONDING lv_det_key3 TO lv_det_attr3.
* Here we call the API to change the details
CALL METHOD zcl_details_api_3=>CHANGE_DETAILS_3
EXPORTING
is_det_attr_3 = lv_det_attr3
it_names = lt_changed_attr
IMPORTING
rv_success = lv_success.

IF lv_success IS NOT INITIAL.
lv_changed_objects-object_name = 'Details3'.
lv_changed_objects-object_id = cl_crm_genil_container_tools=>build_object_id( lv_det_key3 ).
APPEND lv_changed_objects TO et_changed_objects.
ENDIF.
WHEN OTHERS.
ENDCASE.
ENDIF.
endmethod.

entry.title: Re: Protecting Your SAP Landscape: Addressing the SQL Authorization Bypass (CVE-2026-27681)

entry.title: SAP Datasphere: Data Tiering Strategy and Best Practices
entry.content:
Introduction:

Data tiering is becoming a critical pillar of every organization’s data and analytics strategy. As companies move toward AI-driven decision-making and deploy AI agents, MCP servers, and other autonomous capabilities, the value of context-rich, trusted data products increases dramatically. Simply put: high‑quality data is the fuel that powers generative, agentic, and autonomous applications across the business.A modern data tiering (or data management) strategy is about organizing and storing data in a way that optimizes both cost and performance. Data is being generated at unprecedented speed and volume, from business transactions and master data to IoT signals, application logs, surveys, social media, and streaming sources. Not all of this data is equally valuable at all times, and not all of it needs the same level of performance or freshness. The key is to balance the cost of storing data with the cost (and business value) of accessing it.

Different consumption patterns drive different requirements:

  • Real-time operational analytics (daily, weekly)
  • Tactical reporting (monthly, quarterly)
  • Strategic analysis (multi-year historical trends)
  • Advanced AI and machine learning use cases

For each of these, it is essential to understand what data tiering is, which options exist, and in which scenarios to apply which tier. In this technical article, we focus on data tiering in the context of SAP’s Business Data Cloud, with a particular emphasis on SAP Datasphere. We align the discussion with SAP’s latest data and analytics strategy as presented at Sapphire 2026, and provide practical guidance and best practices for designing an effective tiering strategy that supports both traditional analytics and next-generation AI workloads.

1. Business & AI Context:

AI is no longer an experimental add‑on to the SAP landscape; it is becoming the execution layer of the enterprise. With SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents, assistants, and Joule-powered workflows are expected to automate large parts of finance, supply chain, HR, and customer operations. All of that depends on one thing: trusted, well‑managed data with rich business context.

1.1 Why Data Tiering Matters in an AI-First World?

Modern AI in SAP is context-hungry. It does not just need a lot of data; it needs: Who the customer  is across CRM, S/4HANA, and e‑commerce, Which material or location a transaction relates to, What policies or controls apply in a given process or region, How a process (order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, hire-to-retire) is supposed to run. This business context is what SAP brings to AI, and it is exactly what SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere are designed to provide. However, the data feeding AI agents, planning models, and analytics is exploding in volume and variety:

  • Core ERP transactions and master data
  • IoT, machine telemetry, and sensor streams
  •  Application logs and clickstreams
  • Survey, social, and interaction data
  • External market and partner data

Not all of this data needs to sit on the most expensive, highest‑performance storage. At the same time, some scenarios, like Joule agents making operational decisions, cannot tolerate high latency or missing data. Data tiering is how you manage this trade‑off between cost and performance without compromising AI quality and trust.

1.2 From Reporting to Autonomous Decisions

Historically, data tiering conversations in SAP environments focused on:

  • Reducing database size (especially in ERP and BW)
  • Managing performance for reporting and planning
  • Meeting retention and compliance requirements at lower cost

In the Autonomous Enterprise, the stakes are higher. Tiering decisions now influence:

  • Real-time operational analytics: e.g., S/4HANA operational KPIs, supply chain control towers, same‑day profitability
  • Tactical management reporting: month‑end/quarter‑end analysis, scenario planning, rolling forecasts
  • Strategic and historical analysis: multi‑year trends, structural cost analysis, sustainability reporting
  • AI and agentic use cases: Joule agents executing workflows, AI copilots assisting users in SAP Fiori and line-of-business apps,RAG‑style (retrieval‑augmented generation) scenarios pulling context into LLMs, ML models trained on long‑range historical, IoT, or behavioral data

Each of these has a different tolerance for latency, freshness, data volume, and cost. A well‑designed tiering strategy ensures that:

  • Hot data is available at in‑memory speed for agents and operational analytics
  • Warm data supports mid‑term analysis and planning without over‑provisioning HANA
  • Cold data and archives are kept cost‑efficiently but still discoverable, governable, and usable for ML and compliance

2. Data Tiering Concepts & Terminology:

Before going into SAP Datasphere specifics, it helps to establish a clear, neutral vocabulary for data tiering that you can reuse with architects, business stakeholders, and AI teams.

2.1 What Is Data Tiering?  Data tiering is a data management strategy where you separate data into different temperature tiers and storage technologies and core dimensions classifying it:

  1. Access frequency: How often is the data read? Hourly, daily, monthly, rarely?
  2. Update requirement: Does the data need to be updated after initial load? Is it transactional (insert/update/delete) or more append‑only?
  3. Performance & latency:  How fast do queries need to return? Does an AI agent or operational dashboard depend on sub‑second responses?
  4. Data Freshness:  Does the data need to be real‑time, near real‑time, or is daily/weekly refresh enough?
  5. Retention period: How many years must the data be kept for legal, audit, or business reasons?
  6. Data volume: How large is the dataset today, and how fast is it growing?
  7. Security & compliance: Are there specific regulations (GDPR, industry rules) that affect where data can be stored and who can access it?
  8. Business criticality (MOST IMPORTANT): If this data is slow or unavailable, what is the impact on the business and on AI‑driven processes?

In SAP terms, data tiering is part of multi‑temperature data management: you keep the most valuable, time‑critical data on your fastest (and most expensive) infrastructure, while moving less critical data to cheaper, slower layers without losing access to it.

2.2 The Classical 3 Tiers: Hot, Warm, Cold Most SAP data tiering discussions (HANA , BW/4HANA, Datasphere) use the same three core concepts, with slightly different technical realizations. Remote Data Tier is an data access pattern.

2.3 Data Tiering vs. Related Concepts in SAP: It is useful to separate data tiering from other, sometimes overlapping, concepts:

  • Data aging (e..g SAP S/4HANA): Framework that moves older data from hot in‑memory storage to warm disk‑based storage within the same HANA system. It reduces memory footprint while keeping data accessible. This is a mechanism that supports a tiering strategy at the application level.
  • Data Tiering Optimization (DTO) in BW/4HANA: Classifies data in an ADSO as hot, warm, or cold, and maps each temperature to a specific storage technology (e.g., HANA in‑memory, HANA NSE/extension nodes, SAP IQ). DTO is a tool that applies tiering policies in BW/4HANA.
  • Embedded Data Lake / data lake integration: Storing large volumes of structured/semi‑structured/unstructured data on low‑cost cloud object storage (e.g., embedded data lake in SAP Datasphere, or external data lakes). This typically supports warm/cold tiers for large volumes.
  • Virtualization / federation: Data remains in the source system (S/4HANA, Ariba, ECC, BW, non‑SAP DB, data lake) and is accessed on demand via remote tables or views. Virtualization is not a tier by itself, but an access pattern that can implement hot, warm, or cold tiers without physical replication.

2.4 Analytical & AI Consumption Patterns:  Tiering decisions should start from the intended consumption patterns, not from the storage technology. Key patterns include:

  • Real‑time operational analytics:  Examples: same‑day order‑to‑cash KPIs, supply chain control tower, production monitoring.Requirements: hot data, very low latency, typically in‑memory HANA, minimal joins across slow systems.
  • Tactical reporting & planning: Examples: monthly financial close, rolling forecasts, quarterly performance reviews. Requirements: mix of hot and warm data, acceptable latency in seconds; may combine data from replicated and virtual sources.
  • Strategic, historical, and regulatory analysis: Examples: multi‑year profitability, sustainability KPIs, root‑cause analysis over several years. Requirements: heavy use of warm and cold tiers; performance is important but not sub‑second; cost and retention dominate.
  • AI & ML training: Examples: training predictive models, building recommendation systems, anomaly detection, LLM fine‑tuning. Requirements: large volumes of warm/cold data; batch access; cost‑efficient storage more important than interactive speed.
  • AI & agent inference / context retrieval (RAG):  Examples: Joule or custom agents answering questions or taking actions using business data context. Requirements: hot data products with low latency and rich semantics; limited but well‑curated warm data for extended context; some cold data may be pre‑indexed or summarized.

Each pattern maps to a different  temperature mix which later translates into concrete options in SAP Datasphere (local tables (In-memory/Disk), remote tables, object storage (Delta-tables), etc.).

2.5 Temperature as a Policy, Not Just a Location: A subtle but important point: data temperature is a policy, not just a physical storage location.For example:

  1. A table partition might be “hot” for the current year and “warm” for previous years, even though the table itself still lives in SAP Datasphere.
  2. An object in an embedded data lake (Object store) might be “warm” when actively used for ML training, then become “cold” once the model is trained and only needs occasional retraining.
  3. Data can move hot → warm → cold as it ages (time‑based policies) or cold → warm → hot when certain events or AI scenarios increase its business relevance (usage‑based policies).

This policy‑driven view becomes especially important in SAP Datasphere and Business Data Cloud, where you design data products and spaces that must support changing business and AI requirements over time.

2.6 Data tiering characteristics: 

Data TierPerformanceCostData VolumeData UpdatesTypical Business Scenarios
HOTBest / Ultra-FastHighLow-MediumHighOperational reporting, AI Agents, Joule context, SAC dashboards, Open Orders, Current Inventory, Planning, Master Data
WARMMediumMediumMedium-HighHigh-MediumHistorical operational analytics (1–5 years), Supply Chain analytics, Procurement spend analysis, Customer analytics, Finance trending, AI/ML
COLDLowLowMassiveLow/NoneAudit, Compliance, Archived ERP/BW history, Long-term retention, Historical IoT data, Delta lake tables, AI/ML
REMOTEVariable (Source Dependent)MinimalLowVery HighVirtualized access to S/4HANA, BW/4HANA, SAP HANA, Snowflake, Databricks, Azure Fabric, data discovery and low-frequency access scenarios

 

3. SAP Datasphere Architecture and Strategy for Data Tiering: 

Data tiering in SAP Datasphere is built on top of SAP HANA Cloud and the Business Data Cloud architecture. At a high level, you combine local tables, spaces, storage options, and access patterns to implement hot, warm, and cold tiers without losing business context. Remote table will point to directly to source without copying for the remote tier data.

3.1 Key Building Blocks

  • Tenant Sizing/Configuration: Based on the requirements select the storage and memory.
  • Datasphere Spaces: Logical workspaces that isolate data, users, and data storage resources (memory, disk, file storage).You can dedicate spaces to domains (Finance, Supply Chain), regions, or workloads (AI lab vs. standard reporting).
  • Data Builder (Data engineering & Modeling): Remote/local tables, views, data flows, replication flows, transformation flow, Analytic model etc. This is where you design physical/virtual placement and thus tiering.
  • Connections & Data Federation Layer: Connects to SAP S/4HANA, BW/4HANA, ECC, SuccessFactors, Ariba, non‑SAP DBs, data lakes, and SaaS APIs. Foundation for virtual access (remote tables) and data replication the two main tiering levers.

3.2 Storage & Access Options Relevant for Tiering:  Under the hood, Datasphere uses SAP HANA Cloud as a database.

  • Local Tables: Physically replicate and store the data.
  • Remote Tables / Virtualization: : No replication; queries are pushed down to the source (e.g., S/4HANA, BW, external DB, data lake)
  • View Persistency: Persisted views/materializations in Datasphere to speed up complex logic. Often used as a performance buffer between raw inbound data and reporting, instead of keeping everything hot.

3.4. Data Tiering Options in SAP Datasphere

Remote Tier: Remote Tables that point to data in source systems and runs queries via federation without data copy.

Hot Tier: Local Tables in In-memory storage. Local tables physically stored in the SAP HANA Cloud database of your Datasphere tenant (column store).

Warm Tier: Local Tables in Disk storage. Local tables physically stored in the SAP HANA Native Storage Extension (NSE) is a built‑in warm data option in SAP HANA Cloud to keep data on disk instead of fully in memory. It combines disk‑based storage with the in‑memory to lower costs while keeping acceptable performance.

Cold Tier: Local Tables in File storage. Local tables(delta tables) physically stored in the object store (HANA Data Lake Files) of your Datasphere tenant for structured, semistructured, and unstructured data sets.

4. SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

5. Design Patterns & Typical Scenarios

5.1 Pattern 1 – Operational & Agentic Core with Remote, Hot, Warm, and Cold Tiers.

Intent: Support real-time operational decisions and AI agents with the latest business context, keep monthly and quarterly analytics on warm transactional history, and move multi-year data to cold lake storage for audit, compliance, and ML training.

Common scenarios:

  • S/4HANA‑centric processes: Finance, Order‑to‑Cash, Procure‑to‑Pay, inventory, production.
  • A relatively small set of promoted tables: Daily Revenue, Open Orders, Customer 360 Core, etc.
  • Monthly, quarterly, and yearly reporting on transactional history.
  • Operational dashboards, control towers, exception monitoring.
  • Joule/agent scenarios that need real‑time, deterministic context.
  • Audit, regulatory, and historical intelligence use cases.

Architecture view:

Remote tier (Remote tables):

  • Real-time operational data is accessed directly from source applications.This tier is used when the business needs the latest committed state without waiting for replication.
  • Best suited for: Critical real-operational reports, Control-tower dashboards, Time-sensitive AI agents

Warm + Hot, Data warehouse & database ( Local tables-Disk/In-memory )

  • Warm (default): most replicated tables from S/4 and other apps land on HANA disk/NSE. Older partitions of the same transactional tables (12–36 months), used for monthly/quarterly reporting and trend analysis.
  • Hot (explicit): only the most critical slices (e.g., current year postings, open documents, key master data, control tables) are switched to in‑memory for ultra‑fast queries.

Cold tier, Data Lake storage (Local tables-Files)

  • Acts as the foundation of the data fabric and Lake storage holds multi-year historical facts, logs, IoT data, archived detail, and deep history that is rarely accessed but required for audit or ML training.
  • Best suited for: Model/ML training and retraining, Rare deep-dive analytics and Audit and compliance

Design guidance

  • Keep schema, semantics, and business definitions consistent across remote, hot, warm, and cold so that data products can span all tiers seamlessly.
  • Use partitioning on the warm tier so that the active 12–36 month range behaves like “hot” for most analytical queries.
  • Separate your models by purpose:
    • Operational / real-time models should combine remote + hot + current warm.
    • Union models: Remote+Hot+Warm+Cold.
    • Historical / audit / ML models should extend into the cold tier only when needed.
  • Promote only the smallest business-critical slice to hot; keep the broader history warm and the full archive cold.

Example: 1. SAP Datasphere Native Connection with SAP S/4HANA: Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering 

Hot Tier: Current year data (2026)
Warm Tier: Previous two completed years (2024,2025)
Cold Tier: All data older (2023 and earlier)

1. UNIONing Sales Order Hot+Warm+Cold Tier Data, Fact Graphical View

2. Data Analysis View Showing - Analytic Model, Fact Model, Multi-Tier Tables and Replication Flow objects. 

3. Testing

Example: 2. SAP BDC Formation with SAP S/4HANA: Sales Order Data Product with below persisted data tiering 

5.2 Pattern 2 – Cold‑First Datasphere Object Store, Selectively Promoted → Warm/Hot

(Everything lands in the lake, then is “pulled up” by importance)

Intent: Default to landingall data in the cold tier(cheap, scalable lake storage), thenpromote only critical datainto warm and hot tiers based onactualaccess patterns and performance needs.

Common scenarios

  • Cloud‑scale data (IoT, clickstream, logs, partner feeds) combined with SAP business data.
  • Organizations with strong cost pressure and many unknown future use cases.
  • Heavy AI / ML experimentation and data science workloads.

Architecture view

Cold tier as landing zone (Local tables-Files)

  • All raw and detailed data (transactions, events, logs, IoT, external feeds) lands first in the HDLF/cold lake.
  • Acts as, long‑term system of record and single source for all higher tiers.

Warm tier (curated subsets in the lakehouse / warehouse)

  • Frequently used, cleansed, and modelled subsets are loaded from the lake into warm local tables in SAP Datasphere.
  • Characteristics:
    • Structured, governed, optimized for analytical workloads
    • Drives standard BI, tactical reporting, and many AI feature sets
  • Only a fraction of lake data is promoted here at any given time.

Hot tier (very selective, performance‑critical slices)

  • Small number of tables/partitions further promoted from warm to in‑memory hot for:
    • Executive dashboards
    • Time‑critical planning and simulations
    • High‑throughput AI inference and agents
  • Typically:
    • Recent periods (e.g., current month/quarter)
    • Narrow sets of attributes and key metrics

Remote tier (optional)

  • Some data may remain in source systems and be accessed virtually instead of being landed in the lake (e.g., SAP SaaS applications).
  • Treated logically as warm/cold depending on latency and SLA.

Design guidance

  • Treat the cold lake as default; movement to warm/hot must be justified by:
    • Access frequency
    • Latency requirements
    • Business criticality
  • Automate usage‑driven promotion:
    • Monitor query logs on lake and warm tables.
    • Identify top N datasets to copy or materialize in warm.
    • From warm, promote an even smaller top of top to hot.
  • For AI:
    • Training and experimentation pull broadly from the cold lake.
    • Production agents and data products should rely primarily on warm/hot, with controlled fallbacks to cold for rare, deep questions.

Example:  Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering:

5.3 Pattern 3 – Warm‑First Datasphere Core, Selectively Promoted → Hot/Cold

(Default warm in Datasphere, tuned over time up or down)

Intent: Start by loading everything needed for analytics into the warm tier of SAP Datasphere (default disk storage), then rebalance over time: move critical subsets up to hot and aging/low‑value data down to cold.

Common scenarios

  • Typical SAP Datasphere rollout where data replication lands in warm (Disk/HANA NSE) by default.
  • Traditional BI / reporting, planning, and first wave of AI use cases.
  • Customers that want simplicity first, then optimization.

Architecture view

Warm tier as default (Datasphere core Disk Storage)

  • All relevant operational and external data needed for reporting and AI is replicated/ingested into warm local tables.
  • Characteristics:
    • Good general‑purpose performance
    • Simple to model (everything is in one analytical environment)
    • Most dashboards, planning models, and initial agent use cases

Hot tier (promoted, latency‑critical slices)

  • After observing usage and SLAs, promote key tables and partitions to in‑memory hot for:
    • Mission‑critical dashboards and High‑frequency queries
    • AI agents needing sub‑second context
  • Typical candidates:
    • Current‑period transactional slices (e.g., last 30–90 days)
    • High‑value master data (customers, products, locations)
    • Control / security / data access lookup tables

Cold tier (demoted aging or low‑value data)

  • As data ages or is rarely accessed, move older partitions from warm into lake storage (HDFS/object store).
  • Characteristics:
    • Read‑only or minimal updates.
    • Year‑over‑year, long‑term trend analysis, audit and regulatory requirements
    • Occasional ML retraining on full history
  • The warehouse/lakehouse keeps a logical view that transparently spans warm + cold.

Remote tier (where appropriate): For some source systems, keep data remote and blend it with warm/hot only where necessary.

Design guidance

  • Start by designing semantic models and data products against the warm tier; this keeps onboarding simple.
  • Define lifecycle rules:
    • Age‑based: e.g., after 12-24 months in warm, move to cold.
    • Criticality‑based: e.g., if a dataset feeds a critical report/agent, promote its latest partitions to hot.
  • For AI:
    • Keep agent‑critical context (current state, key masters, control rules) in hot or the hottest partitions of warm.
    • Use warm for general analytics, trend analysis, and feature engineering.
    • Store long‑term histories in cold but link them semantically so agents and analysts can still reach them when necessary (e.g., via separate historical data products).

Example:  Sales Order Data with below persisted data tiering:

6. Best practices and Recommendations for Data Tiering:  

Remote tier:

  • Use remote tables when you need real‑time S/4HANA, LoB Apps, or source data with no replication delay.
  • Keep remote queries narrow and push filters and aggregates down to the source. Always filter by time, company code, plant, etc. and Push filters and aggregates down to the source to avoid large data transfers.
  • Avoid overusing remote access for heavy self‑service; replicate to warm if queries become frequent or complex.
  • Use remote + local table together: Keep always‑hot context (small master data, control tables) in local hot and Join remote operational facts with local hot masters for fast, enriched views.
  • If a remote dataset becomes high‑volume or heavily used:  Replicate it into warm and optionally promote the most critical slice to hot.

Hot tier, In‑memory (ultra‑fast, mission‑critical):

When to use ?

  • Data powers mission‑critical dashboards, planning, or AI agents.
  • You need sub‑second to low‑second response and high concurrency.
  • Data changes frequently and is used continuously (daily / hourly).

What to put in hot ?

  • Current period slices of key transactional tables (e.g., last 30–90 days).
  • High‑value master data (Customer, Material, Plant, BP, cost center, etc.).
  • Control and data access control tables used in joins and authorization logic.
  • Persisted outputs of core semantic models and AI‑critical data products (e.g., Daily Revenue, Open Orders, Customer 360 Core).

Best practices:

  • Keep hot small and focused: Frequently accessed, Feeding critical reports or Joule/AI agents, needed for very recent periods.
  • Partition by time and business keys: Make only the most recent partitions hot; keep older ones warm/cold.
  • Materialize complex logic: Persist heavy joins and KPI logic into hot tables (via View persistency/Transformation Flows) instead of recomputing in every query.
  • Minimize width: Only store required columns; move verbose or rarely used attributes to warm/cold dimensions.
  • Protect hot capacity: Use separate spaces or roles to keep ad‑hoc/lab workloads off production hot tables.

Warm tier (HANA disk / NSE):

When to use? 

  • Default tier for replicated SAP and non‑SAP data in Datasphere.
  • You need good performance for 12–36 months of history, but not in‑memory speed.
  • Data is still updatable and frequently used for reporting and planning.

What to put in warm?

  • 1-2-3 years of transactional fact tables (FI, SD, MM, PP, CO, etc.) and less time‑critical master data and lookup tables.
  • Curated, harmonised reporting models that are widely reused.
  • Staging/curated tables used for feature engineering and AI training.

Best practices:

  • Treat warm as your default landing zone for replication.
  • SAP‑managed and customer‑managed data products can be replicated into local table Ideal for Monthly/quarterly reporting, Cross‑domain analytics or Standard AI feature sets and model training.
  • Use time‑based partitioning so:
    • Recent partitions (e.g., last 12–18 months) behave near‑hot.
    • Older partitions are candidates to move to cold.
  • Apply HANA Cloud warm‑store sizing rules and do not let warm grow uncontrolled.
  • Use warm as a “parking lot”:
    • Move data from hot → warm as it ages.
    • Keep still occasionally needed data here before sending it to cold.
  • Monitor usage:
    • Promote warm tables/partitions to hot if they become latency‑critical.
    • Demote rarely used warm data to cold to free capacity.

Cold tier (HDLF / object store):

When to use? 

  • Data is rarely accessed, mostly for audit, compliance, or deep historical analytics.
  • You need very low storage cost and can tolerate high latency.
  • Ideal for long‑term retention and cloud‑scale AI/ML training.

What to put in cold?

  • Detailed line‑item history older than 3–5+ years.
  • Full raw IoT, log, and event streams once aggregated metrics exist in warm/hot.
  • Long‑term snapshots, backups, and regulatory archives.
  • Large external datasets used mainly for ML experimentation.

Best practices:

  • Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store.
  • Design cold primarily for: Historical analytics, large‑slice ML training and forensic investigations.
  • Avoid designs where agents or business users need frequent single‑record lookups in cold.
  • Expose aggregated summaries (yearly/quarterly totals, high‑level KPIs) into warm/hot so everyday analytics don’t touch cold.
  • Implement automated lifecycle: Hot → Warm → Cold rules based on age and usage (e.g., after 18 months move to warm, after 3–5+ years to cold).
  • Customer‑managed data products: Built by customers in SAP Datasphere using Object store mainly from SAP sources (S/4HANA, ECC), non‑SAP data, or other data products. Replicated in Object Store→Data Product→shared via SQL on Files/Delta Sharing. Note: SAP-Managed Data Products are stored in the SAP BDC Foundation Services (FOS) layer, which is separate from the SAP Datasphere Object Store, although both use HDLF technology.

Cross‑tier recommendations (tying it all together):

  • Start from business SLAs and AI needs, not storage and Define clear policies:
    • Remote/Hot = real‑time and daily/weekly decisions (including agents).
    • Warm = regular analytics and planning.
    • Cold = retention, ML training, rare investigations.
  • Promote and demote based on real usage: Instrument and review query stats and Move only proven hotspots to hot, demote unused data to cold.
  • Use partitioning + lifecycle policies + automation instead of ad‑hoc moves to move data hot → warm → cold based on age and actual usage or vice-versa.

Conclusion:

Like we’ve seen throughout this article, data tiering is really about being intentional: put the right data in the right place for the right purpose. Hot and remote tiers serve real‑time decisions and AI agents, warm tiers power day‑to‑day reporting and planning, and cold tiers keep history available for audit and ML without wasting budget. SAP Datasphere and Business Data Cloud give you the building blocks, local in‑memory/disk tables, object storage, and remote access, to implement these tiers consistently. Once you design clear policies and align them with your business data, tables, data products and AI use cases, data tiering stops being a technical afterthought and becomes a core part of your business, analytics, planning and AI strategy.

References:

  1. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE
  2. https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database

entry.title: Linking Academia to the Labor Market Through the SAP ERP Ecosystem
entry.content:
This year I returned to the classroom at the Portucalense University (UPT) in Porto to teach the SAP ERP course as part of the Management Information Systems program.

More than just teaching software, my main goal with this discipline is to build a real bridge between academia and businesses, preparing the next generation of professionals for the practical challenges of the modern labor market.

It’s Not All Technical, It’s Not Just About Software

Working with SAP requires more than just programming skills or a purely technical background. The ERP ecosystem thrives precisely at the intersection of technology and business processes.

Throughout the semester, students dive deep into modern project management concepts and the workflows that drive the corporate world:

  • Finance & Cost Control: Understanding new metrics like FUE in S/4HANA and consumption-based pricing in BTP.
  • Procurement Processes: From the internal Purchase Requisition down to the Supplier Invoice entry.
  • Sales Processes: Managing everything from the Sales Order approval to final Billing.
  • Production & Planning: Grasping the crucial role of MRP and MRP II in managing enterprise resources.

Simulating the Corporate "Day-to-Day" (Trainee Mode)

To ensure students truly absorb the practical impact of the system, the course is structured as a mirror image of a real consulting firm or corporate IT department. Although the students start at a Trainee (beginner) level, the expectations and methodology strictly simulate daily business life:

  1. The Business Pain: We present a real-world corporate scenario or problem.
  2. The Analysis Workshop: Students gather, analyze requirements, and map out the solution (Business Process Design).
  3. Hands-on in the ERP: We validate and test the configurations and workflows directly in the system—journeying through the history of SAP GUI up to the modern SAP Fiori user experience, alongside Clean Core and Grow First strategies.
  4. Presenting to the Client: Students are trained to deliver professional SAP process presentations, defending their final solution to the "Key-User" or client.

Through this dynamic, students not only learn how to navigate the software but also develop crucial soft skills: communication, handling pressure, teamwork, and an executive mindset.

An Overview of the Study Program

To support this journey, we have designed a dense agenda aligned with the vanguard of the global SAP ecosystem:

  • The Technology Evolution: From SAP's foundation in 1972 with R/1 to the predictive capabilities of S/4HANA Cloud.
  • Cloud Transformation: The strategic positioning and differences between GROW with SAP (focused on agility and public cloud scalability) and RISE with SAP (for custom, end-to-end transformation).
  • Architecture & Governance: Managing the transport system (STMS), landscapes (DEV, QAS, PRD), and clients.
  • Modern Extensibility: How to scale and extend features without breaking the core (In-App vs. Side-by-Side using SAP BTP and APIs from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub).

Teaching this course is an incredible opportunity to give back to the technology community. Seeing students connect the dots—understanding how a single click in SAP Fiori impacts a financial balance sheet or a factory production line—is deeply rewarding.

My sincere thanks to Universidade Portucalense for their trust, and to the students for their outstanding energy and dedication in every workshop and presentation!

To my fellow SAP professionals: the future of our market is being shaped right now, and it heavily relies on sharing practical experience within universities.

What has been the biggest challenge in your company when onboarding new Trainees into the SAP ecosystem? Is it a lack of business process understanding or a lack of hands-on practice in the system?

entry.title: Re: We would like to modify the PDF layout generated from...
entry.content:
Hello!

PDF generated from the Manage Customer Line Items app uses a standard SAP delivered form template that can’t be modified directly in Public Edition. Unlike billing document forms (which have extensibility via BAdI and Adobe LiveCycle Designer), the customer line items print output does not expose a supported form extension point. The template is locked, you can’t add subtotals, grouping by customer, or any custom layout directly on top of it.

Building a custom report via CDS View and then attaching a customizable PDF template  is the correct direction in Public Edition. However there are actually three distinct approaches available depending on your output requirement and landscape setup.

Approach A:  Custom Analytical Queries app. The app supports customer-level grouping, reconciliation G/L level, region level, and profit center level, with the option to drill into accounting document level details in the same report. The recommended data source CDS view for customer line items in Public Edition is I_OperationalAcctgDocItem  combined with a filter on account type customer.

Once the Custom Analytical Query is built, it supports:

  • Grouping by customer
  • Subtotals at customer level
  • Export to Excel with all subtotals preserved
  • Export to PDF via the browser print/save function with the grid layout

The limitation of this approach is that the exported PDF reflects the grid layout as-is, it is not a formatted, branded document.

Approach B:  Custom CDS View + Custom Analytical Query + SAP Analytics Cloud Story (formatted PDF output)

Step 1. Build a Custom CDS View. Custom CDS Views in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition allow to create analytical or transactional data models.

Step 2.  Build a Custom Analytical Query on top of the CDS View. Use the Custom Analytical Queries app to create a query from your custom CDS view. Configure customer as a grouping dimension, amounts as measures, and define any restricted measures (e.g. overdue amounts).

Step 3.  Consume in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for formatted PDF output. Custom CDS views and analytical queries can be consumed directly in embedded SAC stories within S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. A story can include tables, charts, and formatted layouts with grouping and subtotals by customer, which can then be exported as a formatted PDF.

entry.title: Result Analysis - Overall Plan Values - Planned Costs and Revenues
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts,

I'm configuring result analysis and i'm having difficulties to understand one of the configuration values of the field "Overall Plan" in the "Planned Values" tab inside OKG3 (expert mode), result analysis.

If the value of this field has the "With Breakdown stucture values" then result analysis takes into account all method for WBS cost planning like Overall/Annual simple cost planning (CJ40), Unit Costing, Detailed Planning (CJR2), Order Costing.

If the value has not the "with breakdown structure values" then results analysis takes into account just the Order Costing (Network order for example). For example if I use the "Annual Plan" = "C", the planned costs in result analysis equals Network costing.

I do not understand what is just the "Overall Planning", value "B". It seems to ignore every planning method. I didn't find any documentation about it.

Do you know where it takes the planning values?

entry.title: Registration of Contact Email Address for SAP CDC Failure Notifications
entry.content:
I understand that an S-user can be registered as the contact person in case of a failure, but is it not possible to send notifications to anyone other than an S-user?
We have created an emergency PERSON_NAME list, but does that PERSON_NAME list need to be registered as an S-user?
If it is possible to register email addresses that are not S-users, could you please provide the specific procedure? We only want to add contact information for failure notifications and would like to confirm if there is any way to do this without using an S-user.

When a failure occurs in SAP, I believe an email is sent to the address set in the following screen:

  • In "SAP for Me", under "Systems and Provisioning" → "Receive Notifications" (see attached screenshot DIGITS-05-28 DIGITS.png)

With this setting, if we want to send failure emails to a different PERSON_NAME list, I believe that PERSON_NAME list must be registered as an S-user. Currently, the old PERSON_NAME list is registered as an S-user.
Since the PERSON_NAME list system will change this time, we need to update the email address. Is it possible to register a PERSON_NAME list without making it an S-user?
I am a super administrator.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Registration of Contact Email Address for SAP CDC Fai...
entry.content:
Regarding to your question, please refer to KBA 2896942 - How Super and Cloud Administrators manage Cloud Notification subscriptions for other users - SAP for Me

Abstract below information for your reference:

**************************************************************************************************************************************************************

Manage subscriptions for users without an S-user ID
Administrators may see users listed that do not have an active user without an S-user ID (e.g. non-users, non-s-users, inactive users, deleted S-users). And the User Type filter allows you to choose between S-users and non-S-users

  • Non-users are users who are not visible in SAP for Me User Management.
  • Administrators likely cannot add new non-users. Instead, the administrators need to create an actual S-user ID for the user first according to KBA 1271482. The new user does not require any authorizations. 
  • In the past, there were various cloud service-specific subscription options that did not require a user to have an S-user ID. When those cloud, subscriptions were migrated to the cloud notification application, the non-users were also migrated. Any non-users that were migrated at that time can have their cloud subscriptions managed by the administrators -- including deleting notifications/subscriptions.
  • Notifications must be set up to a S user or non-s user the notifications can't be set up using a DL E-mail group.

**************************************************************************************************************************************************************

In other words, certain legacy cloud products did not require users to possess an S-user ID. When these cloud products were migrated to the Cloud Notification Application, the associated users were migrated along with them. Therefore, these legacy non-users are still able to use the Cloud Notification Application.
However, apart from these existing legacy non-users, the current Cloud Notification Application does not support creating new non-users. An S-user ID is mandatory for setting up notification subscriptions.
Regarding the emergency mailing list, please note that you must use a dedicated individual email address instead of a department distribution list (DL). There are specific requirements for email addresses when registering an S-user, and generic department DLs are prohibited. Our backend system will perform validation checks on email addresses. Any email containing keywords such as department or office will fail the backend verification.

entry.title: Re: Impairment Posting Error After Missing Fiscal Year Ba...
entry.content: Hi MarkusBredel, Thank you very much for your answer. It appears that adding an interval and an additional depreciation key (ILI) caused a depreciation plan to be generated for 2024, which in turn prevented an impairment charge from being recorded at the end of 2025. In other words, while “adding an interval and an additional depreciation key” is the correct procedure, the resulting “retroactive depreciation plan” means that “an impairment charge cannot be recorded at the end of fiscal year 2025 (because a plan was created in advance that would reduce the book value to zero).” I believe that the sequence “adding an interval and a depreciation key (April 2026)” → “retroactive depreciation plan (April 2024)” is behavior not anticipated by SAP. Best regards, kanki

entry.title: Re: Impairment Posting Error After Missing Fiscal Year Ba...
entry.content:
Thank you very much for your answer.

It appears that adding an interval and an additional depreciation key (ILI) caused a depreciation plan to be generated for 2024, which in turn prevented an impairment charge from being recorded at the end of 2025.
In other words, while “adding an interval and an additional depreciation key” is the correct procedure, the resulting “retroactive depreciation plan” means that “an impairment charge cannot be recorded at the end of fiscal year 2025 (because a plan was created in advance that would reduce the book value to zero).”

I believe that the sequence “adding an interval and a depreciation key (April 2026)” → “retroactive depreciation plan (April 2024)” is behavior not anticipated by SAP.

Best regards,
kanki

entry.title: Re: Safety Stock Alert
entry.content:
Dear Customer, 

In SAP Business ByDesign, automatic alert or email for Safety Stock is not supported, and Safety Stock is monitored only through Supply Planning, this is system standard behavior, please refer to KBA "3736823 - No Automatic Alert or Email for When Safety Stock Level Breach".

As workaround, you can build a report based on the data source Supply Planning Product Exceptions and only display data if the exception type is "Safety Stock Violation". This report can be send or 'broadcasted' to Email recipients e.g. once a day (See Business Analytics work center, view Broadcast).

Henry

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Safety Stock Alert
entry.content: We helped you to accept the answer as solution. You can un-accept it anytime if you have further questions.

entry.title: Re: Joule for Developers (J4D) Not Visible in BTP Entitle...
entry.content:
Based on KBA 3567298 - Inability to View AI Unit Consumption in Business AI application - SAP for Me,

the Business AI application in SAP for Me will only display entries if your landscape is using SAP AI Unit SKU 8019164.

In addition, AI entitlements purchased or signed between October 5, 2023, and August 4, 2024 are not displayed on the SAP for Me Business AI card. These older purchased Units SKUs cannot be shown.

To proceed, please contact the appropriate team as outlined below:
- SAP Account Manager (per 2626344)
- SAP Order Management (per 1660069)
  - Email: sapcontractcenter@sap.com

entry.title: Is It Okay to Use Only RECN Instead of ABST for Reconciliation Accounts in SAP Account Group?
entry.content:
In SAP FI, when configuring Account Groups for reconciliation account purposes, ABST (AP/AR) has traditionally been used. However, I find it more convenient to use RECN only.

Are there any known issues or pitfalls when using only RECN instead of ABST for reconciliation accounts? For example:

  • Impact on Special G/L Indicator processing

  • Restrictions when using Alternative Reconciliation Account

  • Integration issues between subledger (S/L) and general ledger (G/L)

  • Effects on standard reports or analytics tools

RECN is more convenient for me, but I'm concerned about potential hidden issues long-term. If you have practical experience or know of official documentation addressing this, please share.
